summary: Gruber argues against Chris Dixon's claim that mobile apps represent a decline of the web. He redefines 'the web' as anything transmitted over HTTP/HTTPS, making apps and websites peers rather than competitors. Most popular mobile apps are simply native clients for the same web services people use in desktop browsers. Gruber contends that the diversification beyond browser-rendered HTML/CSS/JavaScript is a strength, not a weakness, enabling interaction capabilities that browsers alone could never provide. He dismisses the 'walled garden' concern by noting that app stores don't control the internet or each other, and that many successful mobile-first companies couldn't have existed in a browser-only world. The loss of browser supremacy is just another milestone in an industry always in flux.
key_points: The web should be defined as anything using HTTP/HTTPS, not just content rendered in browsers — apps and websites are peers serving the same services
Usage statistics conflating 'apps vs. web' are misleading because apps like Tweetbot and Facebook constantly render web content and link to web pages
App stores are walled gardens, but they don't control the internet or each other, limiting their gatekeeper power compared to a hypothetical AOL-like monopoly
Companies like Instagram, WhatsApp, and Vine could not have existed in a browser-only world — native apps enabled new interaction capabilities
Browser supremacy was a milestone, not an endpoint — lamenting its decline is like lamenting websites replacing newspapers
money_quotes: I say: who cares? It's all the web.
We should think of the web as anything transmitted using HTTP and HTTPS. Apps and websites are peers, not competitors. They're all just clients to the same services.
We should celebrate, not bemoan, that the web has diversified beyond the confines of browser tabs and the limits of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.
Like water flowing downhill, users gravitate to the best experiences. Saying that we're worse off for the popularity of native mobile apps is like saying water should run uphill.

Lamenting today the falling share of time people spend in web browsers at the expense of mobile apps is no different from those who a decade ago lamented the falling share of time spent reading paper newspapers and magazines at the expense of websites.
App Stores are walled gardens, but the apps themselves are just clients to the open web/internet.
The rise of mobile apps hasn't taken anything away from the wide open world of web browsers and cross-platform HTML/CSS/JavaScript — other than supremacy.
What we've gained, though, is a wide range of interaction capabilities that never could have existed in a web browser-centric world. That to me is cause for celebration.
key_insight: The web isn't dying because of mobile apps — it's expanding beyond the browser, and apps are just native clients to the same open services that define the web.
